figures.gloss: The supreme creator god in Tagalog cosmology. A sky-dwelling caretaker who brought the universe into existence but often remains remote, delegating the daily affairs of the world to lower spirits and ancestral anito.
figures.facet: His name derives from the Sanskrit 'Bhattara', reflecting the deep Indic syncretism of the archipelago. His divine withdrawal leaves a power vacuum entirely filled by localized, highly transactional diwata.
figures.provenance: Boxer Codex (1590); Juan de Plasencia's 'Customs of the Tagalogs' (1589).

figures.gloss: The central spiritual mediator—typically a woman or a feminized man (asog)—who enters trance states to communicate with, appease, and bind the anito. They heal the sick, ensure harvests, and serve as the living repository of myth.
figures.facet: Spiritual authority is explicitly gendered feminine; male shamans could only operate by adopting women's clothing and social roles, completely inverting the patriarchal norms later imposed by Spanish friars.
figures.provenance: Francisco Ignacio Alcina's 'Historia de las islas e indios de Bisayas' (1668); Pedro Chirino's 'Relacion de las Islas Filipinas' (1604).

figures.gloss: A vast, fluid category of spirits encompassing both the deified dead and animistic nature forces inhabiting trees, rivers, and mountains. They are easily offended and demand constant placation through blood sacrifice and offerings.
figures.facet: They do not operate on a moral axis of good versus evil; they are purely transactional. An offended diwata causes illness not out of cosmic justice, but out of personal slight, requiring exact economic restitution.
figures.provenance: William Henry Scott's 'Barangay: Sixteenth-Century Philippine Culture and Society' (1994).

figures.gloss: A colossal sea serpent or dragon in Visayan mythology believed to cause eclipses by rising from the ocean to swallow the moon. Communities would bang pots and drums to frighten it into spitting the moon back out.
figures.facet: It acts as a cyclical cosmic disruptor driven by hunger or infatuation with the moon's beauty, turning celestial mechanics into a participatory, communal noise-making event rather than a static phenomenon.
figures.provenance: Damiana Eugenio's 'Philippine Folk Literature' (1993); early Visayan dictionaries by Mentzel and de la Rosa.

figures.gloss: The beautiful lunar deity of Tagalog and Kapampangan myth. She lost an eye in a fierce celestial battle with her brother, the sun god Apolaki, over dominion of the earth, which is why the moon's light is dimmer than the sun's.
figures.facet: The origin of day and night is framed as a literal sibling dispute over inheritance, physically scarring the moon to explain its lesser luminosity compared to the blazing sun.
figures.provenance: F. Landa Jocano's 'Outline of Philippine Mythology' (1969).

figures.gloss: The primordial Visayan sea goddess and ruler of the underworld. She ferries the souls of the deceased across the cosmic waters in her boat, ensuring they safely reach the afterlife in Sulad.
figures.facet: She collapses the boundary between the ocean of genesis and the river of death. She is both the primordial water from which the world began and the psychopomp at its absolute end.
figures.provenance: Miguel de Loarca's 'Relacion de las Yslas Filipinas' (1582).

figures.gloss: The Kapampangan and Tagalog god of the lower world (Kasamaan) and keeper of souls who lived wicked lives. He commands agents like Manggagaway, who causes disease, to afflict humanity.
figures.facet: Though his name is a syncretism from the Islamic 'Shaitan' via Bruneian influence, his agents function more like localized witches causing specific social ailments rather than bringing absolute spiritual damnation.
figures.provenance: Juan de Plasencia's 'Customs of the Tagalogs' (1589).

figures.gloss: The first man and woman in Tagalog myth, who emerged fully formed from a single massive bamboo stalk after it was pecked open by a primordial bird during a war between the sea and the sky.
figures.facet: Humanity's origin is spontaneously liberated from indigenous flora—emphasizing a symbiotic, lateral emergence from the environment rather than a top-down creation from dust or clay by a divine artisan.
figures.provenance: Boxer Codex (1590); popular oral folklore spanning multiple Philippine ethnolinguistic groups.

figures.gloss: Tagalog and Kapampangan sun god and patron of warriors, named in colonial-derived sources as Mayari's brother and rival for dominion over the earth — the battle between them cost Mayari an eye and left the moon's light dimmer than the sun's.
figures.facet: Sovereignty over the earth is settled not by primacy of creation but by a sibling contest whose wound is still visible each night: the moon's dimness is Apolaki's victory, permanently written onto the sky.
figures.provenance: F. Landa Jocano, 'Outline of Philippine Mythology' (1969).

figures.gloss: The Visayan sky-lord set against Magwayen's primordial waters in the creation account recorded by Miguel de Loarca in 1582 — the sky-and-sea war from which the world of the Bisayan islands proceeds.
figures.facet: Creation here begins as a territorial conflict between two elemental sovereigns rather than the labor of a single maker: sky and sea are rivals before they are complements.
figures.provenance: Miguel de Loarca, 'Relación de las Yslas Filipinas' (1582).

figures.gloss: The androgynous or intersex Tagalog deity of cultivated fields and fertility, presented as among the most beloved of pre-colonial Tagalog gods; invoked at planting with a child held up over the field, in a prayer recorded in Pedro de San Buenaventura's 1613 dictionary.
figures.facet: Fertility's patron holds both sexes in a single body rather than being paired as a couple: the increase of the field is guaranteed not by a marriage of male and female powers but by their union already accomplished in one figure.
figures.provenance: Pedro de San Buenaventura, 'Vocabulario de la lengua tagala' (1613).

figures.gloss: A broad folk-horror category of viscera-sucking, self-segmenting night predators — including the manananggal, who splits at the waist and flies with trailing entrails to prey on the sleeping and the unborn — reported by Spanish friars as the most feared class of being in the islands.
figures.facet: The anti-anito: rather than a spirit to be fed and negotiated with like a diwata, the aswang is purely predatory, its detection and repulsion (salt, garlic, sharpened stakes) forming the defensive folk-practice that anito-era protections were aimed against.
figures.provenance: Francisco Ignacio Alcina, 'Historia de las islas e indios de Bisayas' (1668); Maximo Ramos, 'The Aswang Complex in Philippine Folklore' (1971).

figures.gloss: Primordial deity of the sea and patron of fisherfolk in Tagalog accounts, presented in the popular telling of the bamboo creation as Bathala's rival or coeval power.
figures.facet: The Tagalog counterpart to the Visayan sea-party in a parallel island creation account, never merged into one entity despite the functional overlap — two ethnolinguistically distinct primordial sea-mothers of the archipelago.
figures.provenance: F. Landa Jocano, 'Outline of Philippine Mythology' (1969); colonial attestation is thin — held at the lower claim per the modern popularization of this figure.

figures.gloss: The small carved ancestor-and-spirit images — larauan, likha, taotao — kept in houses, fields and boats and fed offerings of food and oil; the actual tangible interface through which anito and diwata were consulted, and which Spanish friars burned by the boatload in the extirpation campaigns.
figures.facet: The tradition is named for the anito, but devotion ran through carved wood, not doctrine: without a larauan to house it, a spirit had no fixed address at which to be fed, thanked, or blamed.
figures.provenance: Juan de Plasencia, 'Customs of the Tagalogs' (1589); William Henry Scott, 'Barangay: Sixteenth-Century Philippine Culture and Society' (1994).

figures.gloss: The Bisayan (Visayan) moon deity, distinct from the Tagalog Mayari, whose periodic swallowing by the great sea-serpent Bakunawa was the region's own account of a lunar eclipse.
figures.facet: Where Tagalog myth explains the moon's dimness as a war-wound from a sibling battle, the Visayan register instead explains the moon's disappearance as recurring predation by a named monster — two distinct ethnolinguistic lunar theologies once compressed under a single entry.
figures.provenance: Miguel de Loarca, 'Relación de las Yslas Filipinas' (1582); Damiana Eugenio, 'Philippine Folk Literature' (1993).

edges.note: Intra-tradition: The withdrawn, benevolent sky-father contrasted against the active, punitive lord of the lower world.
Intra-tradition: The primordial sea goddess and the colossal sea dragon that resides within her abyssal domain.
Intra-tradition: The human ritual specialist and the vast multitude of nature spirits whose power she channels and appeases.
Intra-tradition: The supreme creator and the beautiful lunar daughter to whom he delegated the rule of the night.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Tagalog supreme god absorbed the Sanskrit title 'Bhattara' (noble lord) via Srivijayan and Majapahit trade networks.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A supreme, distant celestial creator who oversees cosmic order but is accessed primarily through lower, specialized spirits and ancestors.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An overarching sky-father who forms the absolute canopy of the cosmos but rarely micromanages human affairs.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A colossal serpentine adversary that disrupts the natural celestial order and must be actively repelled by communal effort.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A massive indigenous dragon that terrorizes the physical environment and is central to pre-colonial myth.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A predominantly female shamanic tradition where women serve as the ultimate mediators between the human community and the spirit world.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Highly localized nature spirits who demand specific transactional offerings to grant favors or withhold disease.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Territorial nature spirits inhabiting specific bodies of water and earth, fiercely protective of their localized domains.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A psychopomp who ferries the souls of the deceased across a dark, cosmic body of water to the underworld.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A powerful primordial goddess of the sea who commands the deep waters and holds dominion over the souls that pass through it.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A primordial, perfectly balanced male-female pair that initiates the lineage of human history.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A localized sovereign of the underworld who rules over the dead but is not inherently the origin of all cosmic evil.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A lunar goddess closely tied to the measurement of time, nocturnal beauty, and the feminine sphere.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The ascent or release of humanity from within nature itself, arriving in the world fully formed rather than crafted from clay.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The personified deep who commands the primordial, oceanic chaos that surrounds the mortal realm.
GOLD SEAM (substrate): the puwesto continue the anito logic of springs, caves, and rocks as dwelling-places of power — pre-colonial sacred geography persisting under folk-Catholic names.

The sun-war named in Mayari's own myth, now with both combatants present: siblings Apolaki and Mayari battle for dominion of the earth, the fight costing Mayari an eye and permanently dimming the moon relative to the sun.
The Visayan creation war of sky against sea named in Loarca's 1582 account: Kaptan's sky-realm against Magwayen's primordial waters, the generation before the first couple is born.
The sky-sea war of Kaptan and Magwayen is the mythic backdrop from which the bamboo-born first couple, Malakas and Maganda, are pecked into being by a primordial bird.
Two ethnolinguistically distinct Philippine primordial sea deities occupying the same functional slot — Tagalog Aman Sinaya and Visayan Magwayen — never merged into one figure despite the parallel.
The material interface of the anito complex: the carved images that actually housed the same nature-and-ancestor spirits the babaylan negotiated with in trance.

Carved or constructed vessels housing a bound spirit, fed and tended by their keepers — the Philippine larauan and the Kongo minkisi materialize the fetish-vessel joint independently on opposite sides of the world.
The Visayan moon and the sea-serpent that swallows her in eclipse — the region's own lunar-predation pairing, distinct from the Tagalog Mayari/Apolaki sibling-war account of the same phenomenon.

Independent traditions of countless small resident land-spirits requiring ritual negotiation rather than a single cosmic god — the ñuhu of stone and spring, the anito/diwata of place. Structural kinship only.

A documented name-loan only: Kapampangan/Tagalog Sitan borrows the name of Islamic Shaytān/Iblis via Bruneian Islam, but the transactional underworld keeper it names carries none of the jinn-tempter theology of the source.

dossier.essence: The indigenous religion of the Philippine archipelago before and beneath Spanish Catholicism — a family of Austronesian animisms (Tagalog, Visayan, and many others) rather than one church. Its shared core: the world is thick with anito/diwata — ancestral and nature spirits — approached through offerings and through the babaylan or katalonan, the typically female (or feminized-male, asog) spirit-medium.
dossier.cosmos: Cosmologies are layered — skyworld, earth, and underworld(s) — with a remote supreme figure (Tagalog Bathala, whose title derives from Sanskrit via Indic trade contact) above a crowd of transactional local spirits. Illness is commonly read as soul-loss or spirit offense demanding restitution, and several groups reckon the person as carrying more than one soul-component. At death the soul journeys to a spirit-land (Visayan Sulad among others), in some accounts ferried across water; ancestors properly honored become anito who guard their descendants.
dossier.practice: The central rite is the pag-anito: an offering-seance in which the babaylan feasts, chants, and enters trance to host the spirit, negotiating cures, harvests, and safe voyages; animal sacrifice (pig, chicken) and food offerings are standard. Households kept carved spirit-figures (taotao/larauan), and rites marked planting, fishing, birth, and death. Under Spanish rule babaylan practice was suppressed and folded into folk-Catholic forms, where much of it persists.
dossier.canon: An oral corpus of myth, epic, and ritual chant; the earliest written documentation is Spanish-colonial — the Boxer Codex (c. 1590), the relations of Plasencia (1589) and Loarca (1582), and Alcina's Visayan history (1668).
dossier.reflects: Transactional animism under feminine ritual authority — the sacred as a crowd of touchy neighbors managed through etiquette, offerings, and trance.
sources: William Henry Scott, 'Barangay: Sixteenth-Century Philippine Culture and Society' (1994)
Francisco Ignacio Alcina, 'Historia de las islas e indios de Bisayas' (1668)
F. Landa Jocano, 'Outline of Philippine Mythology' (1969)
